protocol_definition.name: Voting
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents answer independently, then cast private ballots; plurality selects the output.
protocol_definition.visibility: Answers are private before voting; voters see all anonymized proposals during voting.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after ballot aggregation; deterministic lowest-index tie break.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. It matches on exact surface-form tokens after text processing (tokenization, lowercasing, stemming). BM25 scoring itself is a statistical weighting of term overlap; it does not inherently model word order or proximity. Positional exact-phrase matching is a separate post-filter or query-parser feature that requires an index storing term positions, not part of the BM25 score formula.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query vector and document vectors. Matching is semantic: paraphrases, synonyms, and related concepts can yield high similarity even with zero lexical overlap. Dense retrieval requires a vector index (e.g., FAISS) and an embedding model.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical | Dense Embedding |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; term-frequency weighted | Semantic similarity; soft conceptual matching |
| Synonyms | No native support; requires query expansion or synonym lists | Captures synonyms through learned representations |
| Exact terms & identifiers | Excellent for rare/code-like terms (e.g., ERR-1042, AX-17) | Unreliable; may map codes to unrelated concepts or miss them entirely |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires positional index + phrase query operator (separate from BM25 score) | No native phrase matching; proximity is lost in embedding |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index, tokenizer, optional positional data; low resource | Vector database, GPU/CPU for embedding inference, model storage |
| Updates | Add/remove documents by updating inverted index; immediate | Re-embed new documents and insert into vector index; model unchanged |
| Latency | Very low; inverted index lookup + scoring | Moderate; query embedding inference + nearest-neighbor search |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch (synonyms, paraphrases); misses relevant docs with different wording | Misses rare codes/identifiers; false positives on superficially similar concepts; catastrophic forgetting on domain terms |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------|--------------|
| Q1. ERR-1042 reset token expired | Exact error code “ERR-1042” | BM25-first | Rare alphanumeric code; dense embeddings likely fail to distinguish it from other error codes. Lexical match is precise. | If the FAQ paraphrases the error without the exact code, BM25 misses it. |
| Q2. I forgot my password and cannot get back into my account | Paraphrase of “password reset” / “account recovery” | Dense-first | Natural language query with multiple synonymous phrasings; dense captures “forgot password” ≈ “reset password” ≈ “cannot log in”. | Dense may retrieve tangentially related account issues (e.g., account deletion) if not well-tuned. |
| Q3. What does product code AX-17 mean? | Exact product code “AX-17” | BM25-first | Identifier with no semantic meaning; dense embeddings will not reliably map “AX-17” to its definition. Lexical match is deterministic. | If the FAQ uses “AX 17” (space) or “AX-17 product”, tokenization must handle hyphens. |
| Q4. Please send my billing receipt again | Action-oriented paraphrase (“resend receipt”, “billing invoice”) | Dense-first | Multiple ways to express the same intent; dense captures “send receipt again” ≈ “resend invoice” ≈ “email billing statement”. | Dense may conflate “billing receipt” with “payment confirmation” or “order receipt” if FAQ covers both. |
| Q5. Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor | Specific action + entity type | Hybrid | “Two-factor authentication” has standard synonyms (2FA, MFA) suited for dense; “contractor” is a specific user role that may appear verbatim. Fusion ensures both signals contribute. | Dense may over-generalize to any authentication disable; BM25 may miss “2FA” if only “two-factor” is in docs. |
| Q6. I am not receiving notification emails | Paraphrase of “email notifications not delivered” | Dense-first | High paraphrase variability; dense handles “not receiving” ≈ “missing” ≈ “not getting” emails. | Dense may retrieve general email setup docs instead of troubleshooting delivery. |
| Q7. Search for the exact phrase “data export completed” | Explicit exact-phrase requirement | BM25-first (with positional phrase operator) | Only lexical retrieval with a positional index can enforce contiguous word sequence. Dense embeddings have no phrase boundary awareness. | If the phrase appears with intervening words (“data export has completed”), positional phrase match fails; BM25 without phrase operator would still score it. |
| Q8. How can I move my workspace information to another account? | Semantic intent of “migrate workspace data” | Dense-first | “Move workspace information” has many surface forms (“transfer workspace”, “migrate data”, “export workspace”); dense captures the intent. | Dense may retrieve “share workspace” or “invite to workspace” if embeddings are not fine-tuned. |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage routing + linear fusion approach is appropriate:

- Query classifier: A simple rule-based router inspects the query for:
  - Presence of alphanumeric codes matching patterns like `ERR-\d+`, `[A-Z]{2}-\d+` → route BM25-first.
  - Quoted phrases (explicit phrase search) → route BM25-first with positional phrase operator.
  - All other queries → route Dense-first.
- Retrieval: BM25 retrieves top-20; Dense retrieves top-20.
- Fusion: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60 combines the two ranked lists into a single final ranking. For BM25-first queries, BM25 rank contributes weight 0.7 and dense 0.3 in RRF; for Dense-first, the weights are reversed. This ensures the primary signal dominates while the secondary signal can rescue misses.
- Fallback: If the top fused result has a BM25 score below a minimum threshold (e.g., no token overlap) and dense similarity below a threshold, return an empty result with a suggestion to rephrase.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations

Risk 1: Code/identifier tokenization mismatch
- Description: BM25 tokenizer splits “ERR-1042” into “ERR” and “1042”, losing the compound code specificity. Dense embeddings treat it as an out-of-vocabulary string.
- Mitigation: Preprocess both queries and documents to preserve known code patterns as single tokens (e.g., `ERR-1042` → `ERR_1042`). Use a character n-gram tokenizer for the code field or maintain a synonym map that expands codes to their canonical forms.

Risk 2: Dense retrieval hallucination on domain-specific terms
- Description: The embedding model was not trained on internal FAQ terminology (product codes, internal process names). It maps them to unrelated concepts, causing false positives.
- Mitigation: Fine-tune the dense model on the 500-document corpus using a contrastive loss with BM25-mined hard negatives. If fine-tuning is not possible, use a keyword-aware dense model (e.g., SPLADE) or boost documents containing exact query term matches in the fusion step.

Risk 3: Exact-phrase over-reliance on positional index
- Description: Q7 requires exact phrase “data export completed”. If the FAQ contains only “data export has completed” or “completed data export”, the positional phrase operator returns zero results.
- Mitigation: When the phrase operator yields zero results, fall back to a proximity-aware BM25 query (slop of 2–3 words) or a span-aware dense model. Log these fallbacks to identify gaps in FAQ phrasing.

Risk 4: Fusion weight miscalibration
- Description: Static 0.7/0.3 weights may be suboptimal for certain query types, causing the weaker signal to introduce noise.
- Mitigation: Implement a lightweight query-performance predictor: if the variance of BM25 scores in top-10 is low (indicating poor discrimination), dynamically shift weight toward dense, and vice versa. Periodically review routing decisions on a held-out query set.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- Create a golden query set: For each of the 500 FAQ documents, write 1–2 natural-language queries that should retrieve that document as the top result. Include variants with synonyms, partial codes, and exact phrases. Target ~100 queries covering all eight query archetypes.
- Annotation: For each query, manually label the top-3 expected FAQ documents (graded: perfect match, acceptable, irrelevant).
- Evaluation runs:
  - Run BM25-only, Dense-only, and the proposed hybrid on the query set.
  - For each run, compute Recall@3 and MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) using the labeled judgments.
- Qualitative error analysis:
  - For each query where the hybrid system fails to rank a perfect-match document in the top 3, categorize the failure: vocabulary mismatch, code misinterpretation, phrase boundary error, fusion noise, or missing content.
  - Tabulate failure categories and identify patterns (e.g., “60% of failures are code-related”).
- Routing accuracy: Manually verify that the rule-based router selected the correct primary route for each query. Measure router accuracy and adjust pattern rules.
- Phrase handling audit: For queries with quoted phrases, verify that the positional phrase operator returns only documents containing the exact contiguous phrase. Count false positives and false negatives.
- Iterate: Adjust tokenization rules, fusion weights, and routing patterns based on error analysis. Re-run evaluation until no systematic failure category remains.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document FAQ system, implement the lightweight hybrid approach with rule-based routing and RRF fusion. The corpus is small enough that BM25 with a positional index and a dense retriever using a pre-trained sentence encoder (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) can both run with minimal infrastructure. The rule-based router handles the critical code/phrase cases deterministically, while dense retrieval covers the natural-language paraphrase queries that dominate FAQ traffic. Fine-tune the dense model on the 500 documents if domain terminology causes embedding failures. This design balances precision on exact identifiers with recall on varied user phrasing, and the offline evaluation plan provides a systematic method to measure and improve quality without relying on external benchmarks or live traffic.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, counting exact token matches in the document. BM25 scoring itself does not model term order or proximity; it has no built-in mechanism for exact-phrase matching. Positional exact-phrase matching must be implemented as a separate post-filter or query-time operator (e.g., a phrase query that verifies adjacent term positions in an inverted index) and is not part of the BM25 score.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense embedding retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vectors using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is measured by vector similarity (typically cosine or dot product). The model is trained to map semantically similar texts close together in the embedding space, enabling soft matching of synonyms, paraphrases, and related concepts without exact token overlap. Exact term and identifier matching is not guaranteed and depends on whether the model has seen similar tokens during training and how they are tokenized.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical Retrieval | Dense Embedding Retrieval |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; no semantic generalization | Semantic similarity; captures synonyms and paraphrases |
| Synonyms | No inherent synonym handling; requires query expansion or thesaurus | Handles synonyms naturally if present in training data |
| Exact terms and identifiers | Strong; exact match on tokens like “ERR-1042” or “AX-17” | Weak; may fail on rare codes, alphanumeric identifiers, or out-of-vocabulary tokens |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires separate positional phrase query; BM25 score alone does not enforce adjacency | No native phrase enforcement; phrase meaning is approximated through contextual embeddings, which can be unreliable for strict verbatim matches |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index (e.g., Lucene, Elasticsearch); minimal compute; no GPU | Dense vector index (e.g., FAISS, Pinecone); requires GPU for encoding and model serving |
| Updates | Incremental index updates are fast and cheap | Re-indexing requires re-encoding documents; model updates require re-embedding the entire corpus |
| Latency | Very low; mature inverted index structures | Low to moderate; depends on approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) index and encoder inference time |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch (synonyms, paraphrases); misses relevant docs with different wording; no semantic understanding | Misses rare exact identifiers; over-generalizes; can retrieve topically related but irrelevant documents; embedding drift after model updates |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------|--------------|
| Q1. ERR-1042 reset token expired | Exact error code “ERR-1042” | BM25-first | Alphanumeric error codes are rare tokens that dense models often mishandle. BM25 guarantees exact match on the code. | If the FAQ uses variant phrasing like “error 1042” without the prefix, BM25 may miss it; mitigated by query-time normalization. |
| Q2. I forgot my password and cannot get back into my account | Paraphrase/synonyms (“forgot password”, “reset password”, “can’t log in”) | Dense-first | High semantic variation in how users describe password recovery. Dense retrieval captures paraphrases that share no exact tokens with FAQ titles. | May retrieve tangentially related security articles; mitigated by score thresholding or BM25 fallback for low-confidence results. |
| Q3. What does product code AX-17 mean? | Exact identifier “AX-17” | BM25-first | Product codes are precise identifiers. Dense models may split “AX-17” into subword tokens and fail to match exactly. | If the FAQ spells out “AX 17” with a space, BM25 tokenization must be aligned; pre-processing can normalize hyphens. |
| Q4. Please send my billing receipt again | Action-oriented paraphrase (“resend receipt”, “email invoice”, “billing statement”) | Dense-first | Users phrase billing requests in many ways. Dense retrieval handles “receipt” vs. “invoice” vs. “statement” synonymy. | May retrieve general billing FAQ instead of the specific “resend” action; hybrid fusion can re-rank by action intent. |
| Q5. Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor | Domain-specific multi-term concept (“disable two-factor authentication”, “contractor”) | Hybrid | “Two-factor authentication” has common abbreviations (2FA, MFA) that dense models capture, but “contractor” is a precise role that BM25 anchors well. Fusion combines both signals. | Dense may over-weight “authentication” and return unrelated auth articles; BM25 anchor on “contractor” mitigates this. |
| Q6. I am not receiving notification emails | Paraphrase (“not getting emails”, “email notifications not working”, “missing alerts”) | Dense-first | High paraphrase variability. Dense retrieval matches “receiving” with “getting”, “delivery”, “arriving”. | May retrieve articles about sending emails rather than receiving; negative query intent detection can filter. |
| Q7. Search for the exact phrase “data export completed” | Explicit exact-phrase requirement | BM25-first with phrase operator | Only BM25 with a positional phrase query can guarantee the exact contiguous phrase. Dense retrieval cannot enforce word order or adjacency. | If the phrase is split across fields, phrase query may miss; index-time position tracking across fields is required. |
| Q8. How can I move my workspace information to another account? | Semantic concept (“migrate”, “transfer”, “export”, “workspace data”) | Dense-first | “Move workspace information” is expressed with high lexical diversity (“transfer data”, “migrate workspace”, “export account data”). Dense captures these. | May retrieve “share workspace” instead of “move”; re-ranking with BM25 on key action verbs can improve precision. |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage approach balances simplicity and effectiveness:

- Stage 1 – Query classification: Use a lightweight keyword-based classifier to detect:
  - Exact-phrase requests (query contains quoted text or explicit phrase indicators) → route to BM25 with phrase query.
  - Identifier-heavy queries (contain error codes, product codes, or alphanumeric patterns like “ERR-\d+”, “AX-\d+”) → route to BM25-first.
  - All other queries → route to Dense-first.

- Stage 2 – Fusion for ambiguous queries: For queries not clearly classified, run both BM25 and dense retrieval in parallel. Combine results using reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) with k=60. This gives equal weight to both signals without requiring score calibration. For queries classified as BM25-first or Dense-first, still run the secondary retriever and use its top-3 results as a safety net appended to the primary ranked list after deduplication.

- Threshold gating: If the top dense result has a cosine similarity below 0.5 (empirically tuned on a dev set), fall back entirely to BM25 results to avoid irrelevant semantic matches.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations

Risk 1: Dense retrieval misses rare identifiers
- Description: Error codes, product SKUs, and version numbers are often split into subword tokens or are out-of-vocabulary for the dense encoder, leading to zero relevant results.
- Mitigation: Pre-process queries to detect identifier patterns (regex on uppercase letters followed by digits, hyphens). Route these queries to BM25-first. Maintain a synonym list mapping common identifier variants to canonical forms in the BM25 index.

Risk 2: BM25 vocabulary mismatch on paraphrased queries
- Description: Users describe problems using words that do not appear in any FAQ document, causing BM25 to return empty or irrelevant results.
- Mitigation: Use dense retrieval as the primary route for non-identifier queries. For BM25, implement lightweight query expansion using a domain-specific synonym list (e.g., “reset” ↔ “change”, “password” ↔ “passcode”) derived from the FAQ corpus itself, not external sources.

Risk 3: Fusion dilutes precision for exact-match queries
- Description: When a user searches for an exact error code, dense retrieval may inject topically related but incorrect documents into the fused results, pushing the exact match down the list.
- Mitigation: The query classifier gates fusion. Identifier queries skip dense fusion entirely. For hybrid queries, apply a boost factor to BM25 results that contain all query tokens as exact matches, elevating them above purely semantic matches in the RRF merge.

Risk 4: Phrase query misses due to tokenization or field boundaries
- Description: A positional phrase query for “data export completed” fails if “data export” is in the title field and “completed” is in the body, or if stemming/tokenization breaks the adjacency.
- Mitigation: Index documents with a dedicated “full-text” field that preserves original token positions without stemming. Run phrase queries against this field. If the phrase query returns zero results, fall back to a BM25 bag-of-words search on the phrase terms as individual tokens.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
Construct a golden query set of 30–40 queries covering the eight query archetypes plus edge cases (identifiers, paraphrases, exact phrases, mixed intent). For each query, have two FAQ-knowledgeable annotators independently judge the top-5 retrieved documents as “Highly Relevant,” “Partially Relevant,” or “Not Relevant.”

Evaluation dimensions:
- Exact identifier recall: For queries containing error/product codes, measure whether the single correct FAQ document appears in the top-3 results. This is a pass/fail per query.
- Paraphrase robustness: For semantically varied queries (e.g., password reset), measure the proportion of top-5 results judged “Highly Relevant.” Compare BM25-only, Dense-only, and Hybrid configurations.
- Exact-phrase precision: For quoted phrase queries, verify that all returned documents contain the exact contiguous phrase. Any document without the exact phrase is a failure.
- Ranking quality: Use Cumulative Gain (CG) with relevance scores (Highly=2, Partially=1, Not=0) at rank 5, averaged across all queries, to compare routing strategies.
- Failure case analysis: Manually inspect all queries where the top result is “Not Relevant” and categorize the failure mode (identifier miss, synonym gap, over-generalization, phrase boundary break). Use this to tune the routing classifier and fusion parameters.

Run this evaluation on a held-out set of 10 queries not used during policy design to validate generalization.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document internal FAQ system, deploy a lightweight hybrid system with a query classifier as the router. Use BM25 with a positional phrase operator as the backbone for exact identifiers and phrase queries. Use a small, efficient dense encoder (e.g., a distilled bi-encoder) for paraphrase-heavy queries. Fuse results with RRF only for ambiguous queries. This design requires minimal infrastructure (an inverted index and a small vector index), handles the full range of FAQ query types, and avoids the cost and complexity of a large-scale learned fusion model. The offline evaluation plan provides a structured path to validate and tune the system before deployment.

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. It matches exact tokens (after text processing such as lowercasing and stemming) and has no inherent understanding of semantics, synonyms, or word order beyond unigram overlap. BM25 scoring itself is not the same as positional exact-phrase matching; phrase matching requires a separate post-filter or query parser that enforces term adjacency and order, which BM25 alone does not provide.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity or dot product between the query embedding and document embeddings. It captures semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and synonyms without relying on exact token overlap, but it can struggle with rare exact identifiers, codes, and precise term matching unless those patterns are well represented in training data.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical Retrieval | Dense Embedding Retrieval | Hybrid Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap (after normalization). No semantic understanding. | Semantic similarity via learned embeddings. Handles paraphrasing and synonyms. | Combines exact token signals with semantic similarity. |
| Synonyms | Fails unless synonyms are added via query expansion or a thesaurus. | Naturally captures synonyms if present in training data. | Dense component handles synonyms; BM25 anchors on exact terms. |
| Exact terms and identifiers | Excellent for rare codes, IDs, and unique terms (e.g., “ERR-1042”, “AX-17”). | Can fail or be unreliable for rare, out-of-vocabulary, or highly specific identifiers. | BM25 ensures rare identifiers are not missed; dense adds context. |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires a separate positional index or post-filter; BM25 score alone does not guarantee adjacency. | Not designed for exact phrase matching; treats text as a bag of concepts. | Phrase filter can be applied on top of BM25 candidate set; dense can boost semantically similar phrasing. |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Minimal: inverted index, tokenizer, basic text preprocessing. Low resource. | Requires a transformer model, vector database or index (e.g., FAISS), GPU for encoding (optional for small scale). Higher resource. | Combines both: inverted index + vector store. Moderate to high infrastructure. |
| Updates (new/edited docs) | Instant: re-index the changed document. | Requires re-encoding the document and updating the vector index. Slight delay. | Update both indexes; BM25 is immediate, dense follows. |
| Latency | Very low (milliseconds) with an optimized inverted index. | Low to moderate; depends on vector index and embedding model inference time. | Sum of both retrieval latencies plus fusion step; can be optimized with parallel retrieval. |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch (synonyms, paraphrasing), no semantic ranking, misses relevant docs with different wording. | Misses rare exact codes, over-generalizes, sensitive to domain shift, can retrieve topically related but irrelevant docs. | Fusion logic can amplify noise if weights are poorly tuned; increased system complexity. |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Q1. ERR-1042 reset token expired | Exact rare identifier “ERR-1042” | BM25-first | BM25 will precisely match the error code; dense may not have seen this specific code in training and could retrieve generic “token expired” docs. | If no document contains “ERR-1042”, BM25 returns empty; mitigation: fallback to dense on “reset token expired”. |
| Q2. I forgot my password and cannot get back into my account | Paraphrasing, synonyms (“forgot password”, “reset password”, “account recovery”) | Dense-first | High semantic variation; users phrase this many ways. Dense captures intent across synonyms. | Dense might retrieve a generic “account settings” doc; mitigation: BM25 boost on exact “forgot password” if present. |
| Q3. What does product code AX-17 mean? | Exact rare identifier “AX-17” | BM25-first | “AX-17” is a precise code; BM25 guarantees matching if the code exists in a document. Dense may confuse it with other codes. | If “AX-17” appears in many unrelated docs, BM25 may rank poorly; mitigation: use dense to re-rank by definitional context. |
| Q4. Please send my billing receipt again | Action-oriented phrasing, synonyms (“resend receipt”, “billing receipt”, “invoice”) | Dense-first | Multiple ways to express the same request; dense handles “send again”/“resend” and “receipt”/“invoice” variations. | Dense might retrieve a “view billing history” doc instead of “resend”; mitigation: BM25 boost on exact “billing receipt”. |
| Q5. Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor | Specific action + entity type (“disable”, “two-factor authentication”, “contractor”) | Hybrid | “Two-factor authentication” has common variants (2FA, MFA); dense helps. “Contractor” is a specific user role; BM25 ensures exact match on policy docs mentioning “contractor”. | Fusion may over-weight one signal; mitigation: tune weights so BM25 anchors on “contractor” and dense expands “disable 2FA”. |
| Q6. I am not receiving notification emails | Common issue, paraphrasing (“not getting emails”, “email notifications not working”) | Dense-first | High semantic variation; dense captures the troubleshooting intent. | Dense might retrieve “configure notifications” instead of “troubleshoot missing emails”; mitigation: BM25 boost on exact “not receiving notification emails”. |
| Q7. Search for the exact phrase “data export completed” | Explicit exact-phrase requirement | BM25-first with phrase filter | Only BM25 with a positional phrase index can guarantee exact phrase matching. Dense cannot enforce word order. | If phrase is not in any document, zero results; mitigation: fallback to dense on the individual terms. |
| Q8. How can I move my workspace information to another account? | Semantic intent (“migrate”, “transfer”, “move workspace”) | Dense-first | High synonym variation (“move”/“transfer”/“migrate”, “workspace”/“data”/“information”). Dense captures the migration intent. | Dense might retrieve “share workspace” instead of “transfer ownership”; mitigation: BM25 boost on exact “move workspace” and “another account”. |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage policy balances simplicity and effectiveness:
- Stage 1 – Query classification: Use a simple rule-based classifier.
  - If query contains a rare code/ID pattern (e.g., “ERR-”, “AX-”, product codes matching regex) → route BM25-first.
  - If query is wrapped in quotes or contains “exact phrase” → route BM25-first with phrase filter.
  - Else → route Dense-first.
- Stage 2 – Fusion:
  - BM25-first: Retrieve top 20 BM25 candidates. If a phrase filter is required, apply it to reduce the set. Re-rank the top 20 using dense embeddings and return top 5.
  - Dense-first: Retrieve top 20 dense candidates. Re-rank by combining dense similarity and BM25 score using a weighted sum (e.g., 0.7*dense + 0.3*BM25) to anchor on exact term matches. Return top 5.
- Fallback: If any route returns fewer than 3 results, merge results from both indexes, deduplicate, and re-rank with the weighted sum.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- Risk 1: Rare identifier not in corpus. BM25-first returns zero results for a code like “ERR-9999”.
  - Mitigation: Implement automatic fallback to dense retrieval on the natural language portion of the query (e.g., “reset token expired”) and return those results with a note that the exact code was not found.
- Risk 2: Dense retrieval over-generalizes. For a query like “disable two-factor authentication for a contractor”, dense may return a top result about enabling 2FA for employees because it is semantically similar.
  - Mitigation: Use BM25 score as a tie-breaker or re-ranking signal; exact match on “contractor” and “disable” will down-rank irrelevant docs. Tune the fusion weight to favor BM25 when exact keyword overlap is high.
- Risk 3: Phrase filter eliminates all candidates. The exact phrase “data export completed” does not appear verbatim in any document.
  - Mitigation: When the phrase-filtered set is empty, automatically fall back to a boolean AND search for the individual terms, then re-rank with dense embeddings. Inform the user that the exact phrase was not found but related documents are shown.
- Risk 4: Index drift after document updates. New FAQs are added but only the BM25 index is updated immediately; the dense index lags.
  - Mitigation: For the 500-doc scale, re-encoding and re-indexing the entire dense set is fast (seconds). Automate a full re-index on every update batch. If real-time is needed, use a streaming vector index and encode new docs on ingestion.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- Create a golden query set: For each of the 50–100 most frequent FAQ topics, write 2–3 natural language queries that users might ask (including paraphrases, exact codes, and phrase queries). Ensure coverage of all eight query archetypes from the routing table.
- For each query, manually annotate the top-3 relevant FAQ documents from the 500-doc corpus. Include partially relevant and edge-case documents to test ranking discrimination.
- Run each query through three configurations: BM25-only, Dense-only, and the proposed Hybrid policy.
- For each configuration, collect the top-5 retrieved documents.
- Qualitative metrics (no invented performance numbers):
  - Success@1: Does the top result directly answer the query? (Yes/No, judged by a human reviewer.)
  - Reciprocal Rank (RR) of the first relevant document.
  - Failure analysis: Categorize each miss as vocabulary mismatch, code/ID miss, over-generalization, phrase mismatch, or fusion error.
- Review the failure categories to tune routing rules and fusion weights. Iterate on the rule-based classifier if systematic errors are found (e.g., a code pattern is missed).
- Conduct a latency measurement under load (simulate 10 concurrent queries) to ensure the hybrid pipeline meets any real-time requirements.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document internal FAQ system, a lightweight hybrid approach is recommended. The corpus is small enough that infrastructure overhead for both BM25 and dense retrieval is minimal (a simple inverted index and a flat vector file with brute-force search are sufficient). BM25-first routing for exact codes and phrase queries ensures precision on critical lookups, while dense-first routing handles the majority of natural language, paraphrased questions. The fusion policy adds robustness without complex machine learning pipelines. This design balances precision on identifiers, recall on semantic variations, and maintainability for a small-scale deployment.

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. It matches exact tokens (after tokenization) and has no inherent understanding of synonyms, paraphrases, or semantics. BM25 scoring itself is not the same as positional exact-phrase matching; phrase matching requires a separate post-filter or query parser that enforces term adjacency and order, which BM25’s default scoring does not guarantee.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense embedding retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vectors using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is computed via vector similarity (cosine or dot product). It captures semantic similarity, handles synonyms and paraphrases, and is robust to vocabulary mismatch, but may struggle with exact term matching, rare identifiers, and precise phrase constraints unless the encoder is specifically fine-tuned.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical | Dense Embedding | Hybrid |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; IDF-weighted | Semantic similarity in embedding space | Combines exact and semantic signals |
| Synonyms | No native support; requires query expansion | Strong native support via training | Strong, with exact-match fallback |
| Exact terms & identifiers | Excellent for rare/code terms (e.g., AX-17, ERR-1042) | Weak unless fine-tuned; may map to similar but wrong concepts | Excellent; lexical component anchors rare terms |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires separate positional post-filter; BM25 score alone does not enforce adjacency | No inherent phrase boundary; relies on encoder’s context window | Phrase filter can be applied on top of lexical results; dense adds recall |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index; minimal compute; no GPU | Vector index (e.g., FAISS); GPU for encoding; model storage | Both inverted and vector indexes; orchestration layer |
| Updates | Fast incremental index update | Requires re-encoding and index rebuild/update; slower | Depends on update frequency of both indexes |
| Latency | Very low (milliseconds) | Low-moderate (depends on index size and ANN) | Slightly higher due to fusion step |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch; misses paraphrases; no semantic generalization | Misses rare codes; false semantic matches; phrase blindness | Fusion weight tuning; complexity; potential for one component to dominate incorrectly |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Q1 | Exact error code “ERR-1042” | BM25-first | Rare, high-IDF token; lexical match is precise and unambiguous. Dense may confuse with similar error strings. | If the FAQ uses “Error 1042” without the prefix, BM25 may miss it. Mitigation: token normalization. |
| Q2 | Paraphrase of “password reset” | Dense-first | High vocabulary variability (“forgot”, “cannot get back into”); dense captures semantic intent. | May retrieve documents about account lockout instead of password reset. Mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “password” term. |
| Q3 | Exact product code “AX-17” | BM25-first | Alphanumeric identifier; dense embeddings rarely represent such codes reliably. | If the code appears only in a table or image, BM25 may fail. Mitigation: ensure text extraction. |
| Q4 | Exact phrase “billing receipt” | Hybrid | “billing receipt” is a specific compound concept; BM25 anchors the exact terms, dense captures variants like “invoice” or “payment confirmation”. | Dense may retrieve generic billing pages. Mitigation: boost exact phrase match in fusion. |
| Q5 | Specific action “disable two-factor authentication” | Hybrid | “two-factor authentication” has synonyms (2FA, MFA); dense handles this. “Disable” is an exact action verb; BM25 anchors it. | Dense may retrieve “enable 2FA” documents. Mitigation: negative signal on opposite actions. |
| Q6 | Paraphrase “not receiving notification emails” | Dense-first | High paraphrase potential (“emails not arriving”, “missing alerts”); dense generalizes well. | May retrieve documents about email configuration instead of delivery issues. Mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “notification” + “email”. |
| Q7 | Explicit exact-phrase request “data export completed” | BM25-first with phrase filter | User demands positional exact-phrase match. BM25 retrieves candidates containing all terms; a post-filter enforces adjacency and order. Dense alone cannot guarantee this. | If the phrase is split across fields, phrase filter may fail. Mitigation: index concatenated fields. |
| Q8 | Long-tail intent “move workspace information to another account” | Dense-first | Complex semantic intent with many paraphrases (“transfer data”, “migrate workspace”); dense captures the overall meaning. | May retrieve “share workspace” instead of “transfer ownership”. Mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “move” and “account”. |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage policy is sufficient:
- Stage 1 (Routing): Classify the query. If it contains a rare alphanumeric token (e.g., error code, product ID) or an explicit phrase-in-quotes request, route as BM25-first. Otherwise, route as Dense-first.
- Stage 2 (Fusion): For all queries, retrieve top-20 from the primary route and top-10 from the secondary route. Merge using a weighted reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) with k=60. For BM25-first queries, weight BM25 results 0.7 and dense 0.3. For Dense-first queries, weight dense 0.7 and BM25 0.3. Apply an exact-phrase post-filter only when quotes are detected in the query (Q7). No external model or vendor is used.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- Risk 1: Rare identifier missed by dense retrieval. A product code like “AX-17” may be absent or under-represented in the dense model’s training data, causing it to be mapped to a semantically unrelated but frequent vector.
  - Mitigation: Always include BM25 results in the fusion pool. For queries with detected alphanumeric patterns, force BM25-first routing and boost exact token matches in the final ranking.
- Risk 2: Fusion weight dominance. A poorly tuned weight can cause one retrieval method to drown out the other, effectively reverting to a single-method system.
  - Mitigation: Use RRF instead of score normalization, as it is less sensitive to raw score magnitudes. Calibrate weights on a small set of hand-labeled queries from the FAQ corpus.
- Risk 3: Exact-phrase false positives. A BM25 query for “data export completed” may return documents containing all three words but not in sequence, misleading the user if no phrase filter is applied.
  - Mitigation: Implement a lightweight post-retrieval phrase filter that checks for the exact ordered sequence in the document text. Apply it only when the query contains explicit phrase markers (quotes) to avoid unnecessary latency.
- Risk 4: Update staleness in dense index. When an FAQ is added or modified, the dense vector index may not reflect the change immediately if re-indexing is batched.
  - Mitigation: For a 500-document corpus, re-encode and rebuild the vector index on every update; the cost is negligible. Serve BM25 from a live inverted index that supports instant updates, ensuring the lexical path is always current.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- Create a golden query set: For each of the 500 FAQs, manually write 1-2 natural language queries that should retrieve that FAQ as the top result. Include variants with synonyms, typos, and exact code queries. Total ~750 query-document pairs.
- Evaluation metrics: Use Recall@5 and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) as primary metrics. Do not use invented performance numbers; report relative changes between configurations.
- Baseline comparison: Run the same query set against three configurations: (1) BM25 alone, (2) Dense retrieval alone (using a small, locally-run sentence transformer like all-MiniLM-L6-v2), (3) the proposed hybrid policy.
- Qualitative error analysis: For each configuration, manually inspect the top-3 failures (queries where the correct FAQ is not in the top 5). Categorize failures into: vocabulary mismatch, semantic drift, identifier blindness, phrase violation, and fusion error. This analysis directly informs weight tuning and routing rule refinement.
- Phrase-handling test: Create a subset of 20 queries that explicitly require exact-phrase matching (with and without quotes). Measure whether the correct FAQ is returned at rank 1 and whether the returned snippet contains the exact phrase. Compare hybrid with phrase filter vs. hybrid without.
- No external sources, vendors, or performance numbers are used; all evaluation is based on relative ranking quality observed on the internal corpus.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document internal FAQ system, implement the lightweight hybrid routing and RRF fusion policy described above. The corpus is small enough that infrastructure complexity is minimal: a simple inverted index (e.g., Lucene) and an in-memory vector index (e.g., FAISS flat index) are sufficient. The primary value of the dense component is handling the long tail of paraphrased how-to questions, while BM25 provides a safety net for exact codes, identifiers, and explicit phrase requests. Start with the proposed routing rules and weights, then iterate on the routing logic and RRF k-value based on the qualitative offline evaluation. Do not deploy a dense-only or BM25-only solution, as the failure modes are complementary and both are needed to cover the query types observed in the eight cases.

[Proposal 1]
1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. It matches on exact surface-form tokens after text processing (tokenization, lowercasing, stemming). BM25 scoring itself is a statistical weighting of term overlap; it does not inherently model word order or proximity. Positional exact-phrase matching is a separate post-filter or query-parser feature that requires an index storing term positions, not part of the BM25 score formula.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query vector and document vectors. Matching is semantic: paraphrases, synonyms, and related concepts can yield high similarity even with zero lexical overlap. Dense retrieval requires a vector index (e.g., FAISS) and an embedding model.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical | Dense Embedding |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Matching behavior | Exact token overlap; term-frequency weighted | Semantic similarity; soft conceptual matching |
| Synonyms | No native support; requires query expansion or synonym lists | Captures synonyms through learned representations |
| Exact terms & identifiers | Excellent for rare/code-like terms (e.g., ERR-1042, AX-17) | Unreliable; may map codes to unrelated concepts or miss them entirely |
| Exact-phrase handling | Requires positional index + phrase query operator (separate from BM25 score) | No native phrase matching; proximity is lost in embedding |
| Data/infrastructure needs | Inverted index, tokenizer, optional positional data; low resource | Vector database, GPU/CPU for embedding inference, model storage |
| Updates | Add/remove documents by updating inverted index; immediate | Re-embed new documents and insert into vector index; model unchanged |
| Latency | Very low; inverted index lookup + scoring | Moderate; query embedding inference + nearest-neighbor search |
| Common failure modes | Vocabulary mismatch (synonyms, paraphrases); misses relevant docs with different wording | Misses rare codes/identifiers; false positives on superficially similar concepts; catastrophic forgetting on domain terms |

4. Eight-query routing table

| Query ID | Important Signal | Preferred Route | Rationale | Failure Risk |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|-----------|--------------|
| Q1. ERR-1042 reset token expired | Exact error code “ERR-1042” | BM25-first | Rare alphanumeric code; dense embeddings likely fail to distinguish it from other error codes. Lexical match is precise. | If the FAQ paraphrases the error without the exact code, BM25 misses it. |
| Q2. I forgot my password and cannot get back into my account | Paraphrase of “password reset” / “account recovery” | Dense-first | Natural language query with multiple synonymous phrasings; dense captures “forgot password” ≈ “reset password” ≈ “cannot log in”. | Dense may retrieve tangentially related account issues (e.g., account deletion) if not well-tuned. |
| Q3. What does product code AX-17 mean? | Exact product code “AX-17” | BM25-first | Identifier with no semantic meaning; dense embeddings will not reliably map “AX-17” to its definition. Lexical match is deterministic. | If the FAQ uses “AX 17” (space) or “AX-17 product”, tokenization must handle hyphens. |
| Q4. Please send my billing receipt again | Action-oriented paraphrase (“resend receipt”, “billing invoice”) | Dense-first | Multiple ways to express the same intent; dense captures “send receipt again” ≈ “resend invoice” ≈ “email billing statement”. | Dense may conflate “billing receipt” with “payment confirmation” or “order receipt” if FAQ covers both. |
| Q5. Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor | Specific action + entity type | Hybrid | “Two-factor authentication” has standard synonyms (2FA, MFA) suited for dense; “contractor” is a specific user role that may appear verbatim. Fusion ensures both signals contribute. | Dense may over-generalize to any authentication disable; BM25 may miss “2FA” if only “two-factor” is in docs. |
| Q6. I am not receiving notification emails | Paraphrase of “email notifications not delivered” | Dense-first | High paraphrase variability; dense handles “not receiving” ≈ “missing” ≈ “not getting” emails. | Dense may retrieve general email setup docs instead of troubleshooting delivery. |
| Q7. Search for the exact phrase “data export completed” | Explicit exact-phrase requirement | BM25-first (with positional phrase operator) | Only lexical retrieval with a positional index can enforce contiguous word sequence. Dense embeddings have no phrase boundary awareness. | If the phrase appears with intervening words (“data export has completed”), positional phrase match fails; BM25 without phrase operator would still score it. |
| Q8. How can I move my workspace information to another account? | Semantic intent of “migrate workspace data” | Dense-first | “Move workspace information” has many surface forms (“transfer workspace”, “migrate data”, “export workspace”); dense captures the intent. | Dense may retrieve “share workspace” or “invite to workspace” if embeddings are not fine-tuned. |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage routing + linear fusion approach is appropriate:

- Query classifier: A simple rule-based router inspects the query for:
  - Presence of alphanumeric codes matching patterns like `ERR-\d+`, `[A-Z]{2}-\d+` → route BM25-first.
  - Quoted phrases (explicit phrase search) → route BM25-first with positional phrase operator.
  - All other queries → route Dense-first.
- Retrieval: BM25 retrieves top-20; Dense retrieves top-20.
- Fusion: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60 combines the two ranked lists into a single final ranking. For BM25-first queries, BM25 rank contributes weight 0.7 and dense 0.3 in RRF; for Dense-first, the weights are reversed. This ensures the primary signal dominates while the secondary signal can rescue misses.
- Fallback: If the top fused result has a BM25 score below a minimum threshold (e.g., no token overlap) and dense similarity below a threshold, return an empty result with a suggestion to rephrase.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations

Risk 1: Code/identifier tokenization mismatch
- Description: BM25 tokenizer splits “ERR-1042” into “ERR” and “1042”, losing the compound code specificity. Dense embeddings treat it as an out-of-vocabulary string.
- Mitigation: Preprocess both queries and documents to preserve known code patterns as single tokens (e.g., `ERR-1042` → `ERR_1042`). Use a character n-gram tokenizer for the code field or maintain a synonym map that expands codes to their canonical forms.

Risk 2: Dense retrieval hallucination on domain-specific terms
- Description: The embedding model was not trained on internal FAQ terminology (product codes, internal process names). It maps them to unrelated concepts, causing false positives.
- Mitigation: Fine-tune the dense model on the 500-document corpus using a contrastive loss with BM25-mined hard negatives. If fine-tuning is not possible, use a keyword-aware dense model (e.g., SPLADE) or boost documents containing exact query term matches in the fusion step.

Risk 3: Exact-phrase over-reliance on positional index
- Description: Q7 requires exact phrase “data export completed”. If the FAQ contains only “data export has completed” or “completed data export”, the positional phrase operator returns zero results.
- Mitigation: When the phrase operator yields zero results, fall back to a proximity-aware BM25 query (slop of 2–3 words) or a span-aware dense model. Log these fallbacks to identify gaps in FAQ phrasing.

Risk 4: Fusion weight miscalibration
- Description: Static 0.7/0.3 weights may be suboptimal for certain query types, causing the weaker signal to introduce noise.
- Mitigation: Implement a lightweight query-performance predictor: if the variance of BM25 scores in top-10 is low (indicating poor discrimination), dynamically shift weight toward dense, and vice versa. Periodically review routing decisions on a held-out query set.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- Create a golden query set: For each of the 500 FAQ documents, write 1–2 natural-language queries that should retrieve that document as the top result. Include variants with synonyms, partial codes, and exact phrases. Target ~100 queries covering all eight query archetypes.
- Annotation: For each query, manually label the top-3 expected FAQ documents (graded: perfect match, acceptable, irrelevant).
- Evaluation runs:
  - Run BM25-only, Dense-only, and the proposed hybrid on the query set.
  - For each run, compute Recall@3 and MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) using the labeled judgments.
- Qualitative error analysis:
  - For each query where the hybrid system fails to rank a perfect-match document in the top 3, categorize the failure: vocabulary mismatch, code misinterpretation, phrase boundary error, fusion noise, or missing content.
  - Tabulate failure categories and identify patterns (e.g., “60% of failures are code-related”).
- Routing accuracy: Manually verify that the rule-based router selected the correct primary route for each query. Measure router accuracy and adjust pattern rules.
- Phrase handling audit: For queries with quoted phrases, verify that the positional phrase operator returns only documents containing the exact contiguous phrase. Count false positives and false negatives.
- Iterate: Adjust tokenization rules, fusion weights, and routing patterns based on error analysis. Re-run evaluation until no systematic failure category remains.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document FAQ system, implement the lightweight hybrid approach with rule-based routing and RRF fusion. The corpus is small enough that BM25 with a positional index and a dense retriever using a pre-trained sentence encoder (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) can both run with minimal infrastructure. The rule-based router handles the critical code/phrase cases deterministically, while dense retrieval covers the natural-language paraphrase queries that dominate FAQ traffic. Fine-tune the dense model on the 500 documents if domain terminology causes embedding failures. This design balances precision on exact identifiers with recall on varied user phrasing, and the offline evaluation plan provides a systematic method to measure and improve quality without relying on external benchmarks or live traffic.

[Proposal 2]
1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, counting exact token matches in the document. BM25 scoring itself does not model term order or proximity; it has no built-in mechanism for exact-phrase matching. Positional exact-phrase matching must be implemented as a separate post-filter or query-time operator (e.g., a phrase query that verifies adjacent term positions in an inverted index) and is not part of the BM25 score.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense embedding retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vectors using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is measured by vector similarity (typically cosine or dot product). The model is trained to map semantically similar texts close together in the embedding space, enabling soft matching of synonyms, paraphrases, and related concepts without exact token overlap. Exact term and identifier matching is not guaranteed and depends on whether the model has seen similar tokens during training and how they are tokenized.

3. Technical comparison table

| Aspect | BM25 Lexical Retrieval | Dense Embedding Retrieval |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------------|

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. It matches exact tokens (after text processing such as lowercasing and stemming) and has no inherent understanding of semantics, synonyms, or word order beyond unigram overlap. BM25 scoring itself is not the same as positional exact-phrase matching; phrase matching requires a separate post-filter or query parser that enforces term adjacency and order, which BM25 alone does not provide.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity or dot product between the query embedding and document embeddings. It captures semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and synonyms without relying on exact token overlap, but it can struggle with rare exact identifiers, codes, and precise term matching unless those patterns are well represented in training data.

For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage policy balances simplicity and effectiveness:

  - Else → route Dense-first.
- Stage 2 – Fusion:
  - BM25-first: Retrieve top 20 BM25 candidates. If a phrase filter is required, apply it to reduce the set. Re-rank the top 20 using dense embeddings and return top 5.
  - Dense-first: Retrieve top 20 dense candidates. Re-rank by combining dense similarity and BM25 score using a weighted sum (e.g., 0.7*dense + 0.3*BM25) to anchor on exact term matches. Return top 5.

  - Mitigation: For the 500-doc scale, re-encoding and re-indexing the entire dense set is fast (seconds). Automate a full re-index on every update batch. If real-time is needed, use a streaming vector index and encode new docs on ingestion.

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage routing + linear fusion approach is appropriate:

- Query classifier: A simple rule-based router inspects the query for:
  - Presence of alphanumeric codes matching patterns like `ERR-\d+`, `[A-Z]{2}-\d+` → route BM25-first.
  - Quoted phrases (explicit phrase search) → route BM25-first with positional phrase operator.
  - All other queries → route Dense-first.
- Retrieval: BM25 retrieves top-20; Dense retrieves top-20.
- Fusion: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60 combines the two ranked lists into a single final ranking. For BM25-first queries, BM25 rank contributes weight 0.7 and dense 0.3 in RRF; for Dense-first, the weights are reversed. This ensures the primary signal dominates while the secondary signal can rescue misses.
- Fallback: If the top fused result has a BM25 score below a minimum threshold (e.g., no token overlap) and dense similarity below a threshold, return an empty result with a suggestion to rephrase.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations

Risk 1: Code/identifier tokenization mismatch

Risk 2: Dense retrieval hallucination on domain-specific terms
- Description: The embedding model was not trained on internal FAQ terminology (product codes, internal process names). It maps them to unrelated concepts, causing false positives.
- Mitigation: Fine-tune the dense model on the 500-document corpus using a contrastive loss with BM25-mined hard negatives. If fine-tuning is not possible, use a keyword-aware dense model (e.g., SPLADE) or boost documents containing exact query term matches in the fusion step.

Risk 3: Exact-phrase over-reliance on positional index
- Description: Q7 requires exact phrase “data export completed”. If the FAQ contains only “data export has completed” or “completed data export”, the positional phrase operator returns zero results.
- Mitigation: When the phrase operator yields zero results, fall back to a proximity-aware BM25 query (slop of 2–3 words) or a span-aware dense model. Log these fallbacks to identify gaps in FAQ phrasing.

Risk 4: Fusion weight miscalibration
- Description: Static 0.7/0.3 weights may be suboptimal for certain query types, causing the weaker signal to introduce noise.
- Mitigation: Implement a lightweight query-performance predictor: if the variance of BM25 scores in top-10 is low (indicating poor discrimination), dynamically shift weight toward dense, and vice versa. Periodically review routing decisions on a held-out query set.

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency (TF), inverse document frequency (IDF), and document length normalization. It treats each query term independently, counting exact token matches in the document. BM25 scoring itself does not model term order or proximity; it has no built-in mechanism for exact-phrase matching. Positional exact-phrase matching must be implemented as a separate post-filter or query-time operator (e.g., a phrase query that verifies adjacent term positions in an inverted index) and is not part of the BM25 score.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense embedding retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vectors using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is measured by vector similarity (typically cosine or dot product). The model is trained to map semantically similar texts close together in the embedding space, enabling soft matching of synonyms, paraphrases, and related concepts without exact token overlap. Exact term and identifier matching is not guaranteed and depends on whether the model has seen similar tokens during training and how they are tokenized.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations

- Description: Users describe problems using words that do not appear in any FAQ document, causing BM25 to return empty or irrelevant results.

Risk 3: Fusion dilutes precision for exact-match queries
- Description: When a user searches for an exact error code, dense retrieval may inject topically related but incorrect documents into the fused results, pushing the exact match down the list.

Risk 4: Phrase query misses due to tokenization or field boundaries
- Description: A positional phrase query for “data export completed” fails if “data export” is in the title field and “completed” is in the body, or if stemming/tokenization breaks the adjacency.

1. BM25 definition
BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. It matches on exact surface-form tokens after text processing (tokenization, lowercasing, stemming). BM25 scoring itself is a statistical weighting of term overlap; it does not inherently model word order or proximity. 2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity (or dot product) between the query vector and document vectors. Matching is semantic: paraphrases, synonyms, and related concepts can yield high similarity even with zero lexical overlap. Dense retrieval requires a vector index (e.g., FAISS) and an embedding model.

BM25 is a bag-of-words lexical retrieval function that ranks documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. BM25 scoring itself is not the same as positional exact-phrase matching; phrase matching requires a separate post-filter or query parser that enforces term adjacency and order, which BM25 alone does not provide.

2. Dense retrieval definition
Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into fixed-length vector embeddings using a transformer model (e.g., a bi-encoder). Relevance is computed as cosine similarity or dot product between the query embedding and document embeddings. It captures semantic similarity, paraphrasing, and synonyms without relying on exact token overlap, but it can struggle with rare exact identifiers, codes, and precise term matching unless those patterns are well represented in training data.

| Q1 | Exact error code “ERR-1042” | BM25-first | Rare, high-IDF token; lexical match is precise and unambiguous. Dense may confuse with similar error strings. | If the FAQ uses “Error 1042” without the prefix, BM25 may miss it. Mitigation: token normalization. |
| Q2 | Paraphrase of “password reset” | Dense-first | High vocabulary variability (“forgot”, “cannot get back into”); dense captures semantic intent. | May retrieve documents about account lockout instead of password reset. Mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “password” term. |
| Q3 | Exact product code “AX-17” | BM25-first | Alphanumeric identifier; dense embeddings rarely represent such codes reliably. | If the code appears only in a table or image, BM25 may fail. Mitigation: ensure text extraction. |
| Q4 | Exact phrase “billing receipt” | Hybrid | “billing receipt” is a specific compound concept; BM25 anchors the exact terms, dense captures variants like “invoice” or “payment confirmation”. | Dense may retrieve generic billing pages. Mitigation: boost exact phrase match in fusion. |
| Q5 | Specific action “disable two-factor authentication” | Hybrid | “two-factor authentication” has synonyms (2FA, MFA); dense handles this. “Disable” is an exact action verb; BM25 anchors it. | Dense may retrieve “enable 2FA” documents. Mitigation: negative signal on opposite actions. |
| Q6 | Paraphrase “not receiving notification emails” | Dense-first | High paraphrase potential (“emails not arriving”, “missing alerts”); dense generalizes well. | May retrieve documents about email configuration instead of delivery issues. Mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “notification” + “email”. |
| Q7 | Explicit exact-phrase request “data export completed” | BM25-first with phrase filter | User demands positional exact-phrase match. BM25 retrieves candidates containing all terms; a post-filter enforces adjacency and order. Dense alone cannot guarantee this. | If the phrase is split across fields, phrase filter may fail. Mitigation: index concatenated fields. |
| Q8 | Long-tail intent “move workspace information to another account” | Dense-first | Complex semantic intent with many paraphrases (“transfer data”, “migrate workspace”); dense captures the overall meaning. | May retrieve “share workspace” instead of “transfer ownership”. Mitigation: fuse with BM25 on “move” and “account”. |

5. Lightweight hybrid routing/fusion policy
For a 500-document FAQ corpus, a two-stage policy is sufficient:
- Stage 1 (Routing): Classify the query. If it contains a rare alphanumeric token (e.g., error code, product ID) or an explicit phrase-in-quotes request, route as BM25-first. Otherwise, route as Dense-first.
- Stage 2 (Fusion): For all queries, retrieve top-20 from the primary route and top-10 from the secondary route. Merge using a weighted reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) with k=60. For BM25-first queries, weight BM25 results 0.7 and dense 0.3. For Dense-first queries, weight dense 0.7 and BM25 0.3. Apply an exact-phrase post-filter only when quotes are detected in the query (Q7). No external model or vendor is used.

6. Three or more failure risks and mitigations
- Risk 1: Rare identifier missed by dense retrieval. A product code like “AX-17” may be absent or under-represented in the dense model’s training data, causing it to be mapped to a semantically unrelated but frequent vector.
  - Mitigation: Always include BM25 results in the fusion pool. For queries with detected alphanumeric patterns, force BM25-first routing and boost exact token matches in the final ranking.
- Risk 2: Fusion weight dominance. A poorly tuned weight can cause one retrieval method to drown out the other, effectively reverting to a single-method system.
  - Mitigation: Use RRF instead of score normalization, as it is less sensitive to raw score magnitudes. Calibrate weights on a small set of hand-labeled queries from the FAQ corpus.
- Risk 3: Exact-phrase false positives. A BM25 query for “data export completed” may return documents containing all three words but not in sequence, misleading the user if no phrase filter is applied.
  - Mitigation: Implement a lightweight post-retrieval phrase filter that checks for the exact ordered sequence in the document text. Apply it only when the query contains explicit phrase markers (quotes) to avoid unnecessary latency.
- Risk 4: Update staleness in dense index. When an FAQ is added or modified, the dense vector index may not reflect the change immediately if re-indexing is batched.
  - Mitigation: For a 500-document corpus, re-encode and rebuild the vector index on every update; the cost is negligible. Serve BM25 from a live inverted index that supports instant updates, ensuring the lexical path is always current.

7. Qualitative offline evaluation plan
- Create a golden query set: For each of the 500 FAQs, manually write 1-2 natural language queries that should retrieve that FAQ as the top result. Include variants with synonyms, typos, and exact code queries. Total ~750 query-document pairs.
- Evaluation metrics: Use Recall@5 and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) as primary metrics. Do not use invented performance numbers; report relative changes between configurations.
- Baseline comparison: Run the same query set against three configurations: (1) BM25 alone, (2) Dense retrieval alone (using a small, locally-run sentence transformer like all-MiniLM-L6-v2), (3) the proposed hybrid policy.
- Qualitative error analysis: For each configuration, manually inspect the top-3 failures (queries where the correct FAQ is not in the top 5). Categorize failures into: vocabulary mismatch, semantic drift, identifier blindness, phrase violation, and fusion error. This analysis directly informs weight tuning and routing rule refinement.
- Phrase-handling test: Create a subset of 20 queries that explicitly require exact-phrase matching (with and without quotes). Measure whether the correct FAQ is returned at rank 1 and whether the returned snippet contains the exact phrase. Compare hybrid with phrase filter vs. hybrid without.
- No external sources, vendors, or performance numbers are used; all evaluation is based on relative ranking quality observed on the internal corpus.

8. Final recommendation for 500 FAQ documents
For a 500-document internal FAQ system, implement the lightweight hybrid routing and RRF fusion policy described above. The corpus is small enough that infrastructure complexity is minimal: a simple inverted index (e.g., Lucene) and an in-memory vector index (e.g., FAISS flat index) are sufficient. The primary value of the dense component is handling the long tail of paraphrased how-to questions, while BM25 provides a safety net for exact codes, identifiers, and explicit phrase requests. Start with the proposed routing rules and weights, then iterate on the routing logic and RRF k-value based on the qualitative offline evaluation. Do not deploy a dense-only or BM25-only solution, as the failure modes are complementary and both are needed to cover the query types observed in the eight cases.

agent_task.prompt:
Explain BM25 lexical retrieval, dense embedding retrieval, and a hybrid approach for an internal FAQ system containing 500 documents. Compare matching behavior, synonyms, exact terms and identifiers, exact-phrase handling, data/infrastructure needs, updates, latency, and common failure modes. Be precise that BM25 scoring itself is not the same as positional exact-phrase matching.

Analyze all eight fixed query cases and assign each a BM25-first, Dense-first, or Hybrid route with a brief rationale:
Q1. ERR-1042 reset token expired
Q2. I forgot my password and cannot get back into my account
Q3. What does product code AX-17 mean?
Q4. Please send my billing receipt again
Q5. Disable two-factor authentication for a contractor
Q6. I am not receiving notification emails
Q7. Search for the exact phrase "data export completed"
Q8. How can I move my workspace information to another account?

Then design a lightweight routing/fusion policy for this corpus, provide at least three failure risks with mitigations, and propose a qualitative offline evaluation plan. Do not use external sources, vendors, or invented performance numbers.
